coredata.dc:title: Elucidating the role of etchants induced photo- and electro-activity in holey graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots composites for textile electrode applications
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Kyung Chul
Ali, Mumtaz
Sahito, Iftikhar Ali
Noh, Jung Woo
Jeong, Sung Hoon
coredata.dc:description: Holey graphene oxide (HGO) can unlock the potential of graphene oxide (GO) by providing high surface area, increased surface reactivity, and fast electrolyte flow. Generally, HGOs are prepared by etching GO, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are formed as a byproduct during the etching. GQDs are active materials to increase charge mobility and reduce charge recombination. Previous studies employed strong and high boiling point etchants (e.g., H2SO4), and a filtration process is required for purification. GQDs remained in the filtrate and were lost during the purification because of their small size. To avoid the wastage of GQDs, we tested the low boiling point etchants, which can be separated via vacuum drying to retain GQDs. Considering easy recovery, H2O2, NH4OH, and HNO3 were used for etching, and the resulting composite of GQDs and HGO was characterized and tested for photocatalytic and electrocatalytic activity. The active composite materials were loaded on carbon cloth and reduced to fabricate a stable electrode for performance evaluation. Amongst all etchants, NH4OH could generate the highest surface roughness and nitrogen doping. The NH4OH etched HGO & GQDs composite demonstrated excellent photocatalytic and electrocatalytic activity due to its enhanced surface reactivity, despite its reduced surface area. Compared to negligible photocatalysis of rGO, the optimized composite of GQDs and HGO showed nearly complete dye degradation in 4 h.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanomaterials
Etching
Graphene quantum dots
Holey graphene
Textile substrates

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) Grant funded by the Korea government (MIST) (No. 2022R1A2C1011718 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration showing electrolyte diffusion kinetics through staked rGO structure and HGO+GQDs mixture. (b) Experimental design for the fabrication of GQDs+HGO coated carbon cloth electrode.
Initially rGO was etched using a hydrothermal method, afterwards the composite of HGO and GQDs was coated on the fabric for photo- and electrocatalytic applications.
TEM images and SAED pattern of (a) rGO, (b) HGO, (c) HGO1, and (d) HGO2.
SEM images of the top surface, (a) rGO, (b) HGO, (c) HGO1, and (d) HGO2.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) Raman spectra of rGO, HGO, HGO1, and HGO2.
(a) Chemical composition analyzed via XPS and (b) BET analysis to measure the surface area of rGO, HGO, HGO1, and HGO2.
(a) UV-Vis absorbance spectra, and (b) PL spectra of rGO, HGO, HGO1, and HGO2.
(a) Photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue dye using different electrodes, and (b) after 4 h of photodegradation.
Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) measurements (a) E onset and (b) E cut off.
(a) Comparison of electrocatalytic activity of different electrode materials. Thermal conductivity analysis of the samples after (b) heat pressing and (c & d) on hot-plate.
Starting materials for the synthesis of HGOS via a hydrothermal process.

head.ce:title: Elucidating the role of etchants induced photo- and electro-activity in holey graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots composites for textile electrode applications

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Human-Tech Convergence Program, Department of Organic and Nano Engineering, Hanyang University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Holey graphene oxide (HGO) can unlock the potential of graphene oxide (GO) by providing high surface area, increased surface reactivity, and fast electrolyte flow. Generally, HGOs are prepared by etching GO, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are formed as a byproduct during the etching. GQDs are active materials to increase charge mobility and reduce charge recombination. Previous studies employed strong and high boiling point etchants (e.g., H2SO4), and a filtration process is required for purification. GQDs remained in the filtrate and were lost during the purification because of their small size. To avoid the wastage of GQDs, we tested the low boiling point etchants, which can be separated via vacuum drying to retain GQDs. Considering easy recovery, H2O2, NH4OH, and HNO3 were used for etching, and the resulting composite of GQDs and HGO was characterized and tested for photocatalytic and electrocatalytic activity. The active composite materials were loaded on carbon cloth and reduced to fabricate a stable electrode for performance evaluation. Amongst all etchants, NH4OH could generate the highest surface roughness and nitrogen doping. The NH4OH etched HGO & GQDs composite demonstrated excellent photocatalytic and electrocatalytic activity due to its enhanced surface reactivity, despite its reduced surface area. Compared to negligible photocatalysis of rGO, the optimized composite of GQDs and HGO showed nearly complete dye degradation in 4 h.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Composite film consisting of holey graphene oxide sheets (HGO) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were studied for photo- and electro-catalysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Nano-carbons have emerged as potential metal-free catalysts due to their high chemical stability, lower cost, and tunable chemistry. To compete with traditional noble metal catalysts, the catalytic performance of carbon materials has been improved by various modifications, such as doping nonmetals, introducing intrinsic defects, and designing novel configurations [1]. The activity of reduced graphene oxide (rGO) strongly depends on modifications, which can effectively enhance surface reactivity and increase surface area [2]. The defect-rich edge sites of rGO are more active than its basal planes. The dangling bonds at the active sites serve as a center for mass adsorption/catalysis reactions, facilitating hetero-atom doping [3]. One of the emerging techniques to enhance the reactivity of smooth basal planes of rGO is to introduce defects or holes in the inert basal structure to form holey graphene oxide (HGO) [3]. Generally, HGOs are prepared by etching rGO to increase the reactivity, as excessive defect sites on the edges around the holes serve as an active center. Moreover, the HGO has a relatively weak π-π interaction, preventing the restacking of the HGO sheets [4]. Holes also reduce the electrolyte/media flow path by increasing the accessible area for fast mass transport ( Fig. 1). These attractive attributes of HGO enhance the catalytic activity of HGO for energy and water treatment applications.

Organic waste in water resources has drastically increased and reached a life-threatening limit. Photocatalysis holds great promise to solve this problem, as it utilizes free solar energy to degrade organic molecules in water [5]. Typically, the photocatalyst material absorbs sunlight and produces electron-hole pair, which reacts with surrounding water to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) [6]. These highly reactive ROS can reduce/oxidize the organic contaminants. Traditional photocatalysts are metal oxides; however, limited light absorption spectrum, metal-ion leaching, and relatively high cost of metal oxides have sparked interest in metal-free catalysts. In addition, the passivation of these photocatalysts on porous substrates is a solution to improve their stability and recyclability and mitigate secondary pollution. Textile substrates are the suitable option due to their high porosity and surface area for aggregation-free assembly of photocatalysts [7–12].

Carbon fiber electrodes are preferred over natural fiber substrates as their conductive nature enables their application in electro- and photo-catalytic applications [13,14]. In addition, carbon fiber is the finest fiber among all textile fibers, hence offering the highest surface area. Also, the inorganic nature of carbon fibers renders superb corrosion resistance in aqueous solutions compared to metal mesh substrates [15]. The high conductivity of both rGO and carbon fiber is also suitable for thermal diffusion and heat absorption applications. However, the effects of surface functionality and associated composite films on their thermal response are a less explored area.

The properties of modified rGO sheets are strongly influenced by their orientations, as the reaction rate is limited by the complex interactions of polluted water and stacked rGO structure [16]. Only the topmost surface is available for the reaction in the stacked rGO sheets, hence the overall activity is significantly lower than the theoretical surface area. Doping heteroatom, changing orientations, and creating a larger surface of rGO are expected to unlock the potential of rGO [16]. These modifications enhance the surface reactivity and alter the interfacial interactions responsible for restacking, resulting in better catalytic activity [16,17]. In this regard, etching rGO to produce graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and change their orientations have been studied recently. Here, GQDs served as an active semiconductor material and rGO support provided charge mobility to reduce the charge recombination [17]. The rGO also serves as a co-catalyst by accelerating the catalytic activity via photothermal effect. Apart from light-driven dye degradation, the electrocatalytic activity of the GQDs-rGO composite further boosts the charge separation and associated catalysis [7,18]. In the etching process to prepare HGOs, GQDs are formed as a byproduct. HGO is the etched rGO which has excessive holes in the planer structure. These holes are formed due to the erosion of amorphous carbon domains, along with creating the active edge sites on hole boundaries. However, the composite structure of HGO-GQDs has not been studied for photo- and electro-catalysis. In addition, the role of the etchant in the structure of HGO and associated electro/photocatalysis have not been studied in detail.

Hydrothermal method provides ease of processing for the high temperature and high-pressure reactions. As a matter of fact, the heat energy is not wasted under the typical closed conditions of the hydrothermal technique and the process is catalyzed, compared to condenser reflux system. High temperature conditions are also useful for the partial thermal reduction of GO. Hence, lower consumption of reducing agent will be required for complete reduction to rGO [19].

Considering the above research gap, this work compared the catalytic activity of HGO sheets synthesized using different etchant mixtures, including HNO3, H2O2, and NH4OH. Moreover, GQDs formed during the etching process were kept in the HGO mix. Composite structure (HGO and GQDs) was then coated on the carbon cloth and chemically reduced to stabilize it on the fabric surface. Benefiting from the holey structure, N-doping, and formation of GQDs after etching, significantly improved photocatalysis and electrocatalysis were observed. Amongst all etchants, NH4OH etching provided the best catalytic activities, which were related to the higher content of GQDs, higher conductivity, and porous surface morphology. In addition, the lower boiling point of NH4OH makes it easy to recover from the reaction mixture, hence offering a green synthesis route for scalable production.

For the synthesis of GO, graphite powder with a particle size of less than 100 µm (Asbury Carbons, USA) was used as the starting material. Other chemicals, including sulfuric acid (99 %), KMnO4, H2O2 (35 %), HCl (37 %), NH4OH (30 %), HNO3 (70 %), and N2H4 (50 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Common solvents and laboratory chemicals were purchased from a local market. Carbon cloth (W0S1011) with an aerial density of 130 g/m2 was purchased from NARA Cell-Tech Corp (South Korea). For the synthesis of GO, a modified Hummer’s method was followed [13]. Table 1 lists the low boiling point etchants used in this study. All samples were prepared in a PTFE vial (300 mL capacity) with the same concentration of GO (0.5 g in 200 mL). Homogenous dispersion of GO was achieved by magnetic stirring for one hour and water bath sonication for 1 h. Three samples were synthesized by adding 10 mL H2O2 (labeled as HGO), 10 mL H2O2 with 20 mL NH4OH (labeled as HGO1), and 10 mL H2O2 with 40 mL HNO3 (labeled as HGO2), respectively (Table 1). The reactant mixture was then transferred to a titanium chamber for heating at 100 °C for 4 h ( Fig. 2). After the hydrothermal reaction, the solvents were removed using a high vacuum rotary evaporator, and the mixture of GQDs and HGO was obtained. This mixture was redispersed in water and loaded on carbon cloth via a dip-dry method. Partial reduction of HGO occurs during the thermal treatment process; however, the complete chemical reduction was carried out using 0.1 M hydrazine monohydrate solution, under 70 °C for 45 min [19,20].

The morphology and structure of the materials were investigated by a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, JSM-6700 F, JEOL Co.) and a field-emission scanning transmission electron microscope (FE-STEM, JEM-2100 F, JEOL Co.). The surface areas were calculated using Braunet Emit Teller (BET) surface area analyzer (Micromeritics system) equipped with software (Autosorb-iQ/MP, Quantachrome Instrument Co.). The structural characteristics of the films were investigated using Raman spectrometry (DXR-3xi, Thermo Fisher Co.) with an excitation wavelength of 455 nm. The elemental composition and chemical groups in the modified carbon heterostructures were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha+, Thermo Fisher Co.). The XPS measurement was carried out with a scan number of 10, beam size of 100 µm, phase energy of 50.0 eV, and step size of 0.10 eV. UV–vis absorption spectra of the photocatalysts before and after dye degradation were obtained using a UV-1650PC spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co.). Photoluminesence (PL) spectroscopy was performed using a FluoTime 300 (PicoQuant Co.) with an excitation wavelength of 265 nm. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed using an ultimate electrochemical workstation (VSP, Biologic Co.). The thermal conductivity was measured by a thermal imaging camera (TE-Q1, Thermal Expert Co.) equipped with Thermal Expert android software.

Restacking rGO sheets brings a significant obstacle in utilizing their high surface area, as unwanted stacked structure renders sluggish reaction kinetics [21]. HGO derivatives offer higher surface reactivity and better electrolyte mobility and unlock the full potential of rGO [22]. Despite the high reactivity of rGO, the diffusion rate limits the overall catalysis rate. To overcome this problem, the HGO and its lower derivative GQDs can substantially enhance the surface reactivity and electrolyte/media flow [22]. The formation of porous structure and the improved flow of media in HGO and GQDs composite film is shown schematically in Fig. 1. The etchants used during the hydrothermal treatment have strong oxidizing ability and can selectively cut the amorphous domains of rGO, which are relatively susceptible to oxidation [19,22]. Depending on the concentration of the oxidant and the temperature of the etching process, the degree of oxidation and the structure of the holes can be modulated. Considering this, various etchants were used to prepare different HGO configurations at 100 °C for 4 h. Finally, the HGO and GQDs mixture was loaded on the carbon cloth (CC) to fabricate flexible electrodes for photocatalytic and electrocatalytic applications (Fig. 2).

Here low boiling point oxidizing agents are used as etchants because they can be easily separated by a rotary evaporator. By doing so, the residual chemicals are recovered, reducing the adverse environmental impacts. More importantly, the evaporation process can retain the highly active GQDs in the HGO. In contrast, previous works utilized strong and high boiling point etchant (e.g., H2SO4), and a filtration process was required for purification. During the purification, GQDs remained in the filtrate and were lost because of their small size.

In order to compare the difference in roughness, AFM analysis was carried out in tapping mode for 5 × 5 µm area. Similar to TEM analysis, the roughness of HGO was lower than HGO1 and HGO2 as shown in supplementary Fig. S1. Also, HGO showed higher tendency of film formation and planer structure in film form. On the other hand, the HGO1 does not only show planer structure, but aggregated particulates were also observed. Compared to all these etched samples, the HGO2 showed a crumpled structure and least characteristics of planer sheets.

TEM images of the rGO before and after etching are shown in Fig. 3. Slightly folded planes are observed in the pristine rGO (Column a). Compared to the low-resolution image (Fig. 3 (a-1)), the smooth planer structures are more prominent in a high-resolution image (Fig. 3 (a-2)). Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) shows the defect-free basal planes in pristine rGO (Fig. 3 (a-3)). In contrast, H2O2 treatment create holes in rGO planes and generate extremely small GQDs (Fig. 3 (b)). At low resolutions (Fig. 3 (b-1)), the distribution of the holes is uniform, and the size of the individual hole is in the range of 10–50 nm. At high resolution (Fig. 3 (b-2) and (b-1) inset), the quasi-circular morphology of etched holes is distinct [23]. Overall, the shape of the holes in HGO is random due to the spontaneous etching process. In addition to holes, extremely small GQDs are also distributed uniformly on the surface of HGO, which serve as a functional spacer for the 2D sheets. Compared to the hole size, the size of GQDs was significantly smaller, i.e., in the range of 2–5 nm, implying that multiple GQDs can be formed from a single hole during the etching process. The SAED pattern in Fig. 3 (b-3) shows amorphous rings, suggesting a large number of defects in HGO, as compared to rGO [16].

Similarly, the rGO etched by H2O2 and NH4OH (HGO1) show both HGO sheets and GQDs (Fig. 3 (c)). In Fig. 3 (c-1 and 2), adding NH4OH leads to relatively larger holes (10–60 nm), suggesting the higher reactivity of the mixture of H2O2 and NH4OH. Both HGO1 and associated GQDs (Fig. 3 (c-1) inset) are amorphous in nature [3]. In the mixture of HGO1 and GQDs, it is difficult to identify GQDs. However, after HGO1 is filtered, GQDs emerge with small size and uniform distribution. Compared to pristine rGO, the SAED pattern of HGO1 confirms its defect-rich morphology [3]. On the other hand, HGO2 shows folded morphology with ultra-small holes in the basal planes (Fig. 3 (d-1 and 2)). The GQDs formed in HGO2 are found at the cutting of HGO edges, as highlighted in Fig. 3 (d-1)·H2O2 is basic in nature, which slightly neutralizes the of effect of nitric acid, resulting in the weaker nature of the etchant (10 mL H2O2 and 40 mL HNO3). Therefore, it tends to remove the amorphous domains selectively instead of creating holes in the basal planes. Etched basal planes can be observed at high resolution, as shown in the inset of Fig. 3 (d-2). Removal of amorphous domains during etching is also evident from the brighter pattern of SAED (Fig. 3 (d-3)).

The low-resolution SEM observation shows the morphologies of the electrodes coated with different HGO and GQDs mixtures ( Fig. 4). The pores formed after etching are not visible using low-resolution SEM, whereas a significant difference in surface morphology is observed [24]. Nano-holes observed in the TEM are not clear in SEM because of stacked thin nano-sheets of HGO in film form; however, holes existence of holes effects the properties of the HGO-based sheets [24]. As expected, the rGO-coated electrode has a smooth surface, as shown in Fig. 4 (a). In contrast, surface roughness is increased in all etched samples. HGO and HGO2 show similar folded graphene structures in Fig. 4 (b) and (d), respectively. Comparatively, a rougher surface is found in HGO1 with an earthworm-like structure. Higher roughness provides a larger interface for interaction of HGO film and organic pollutant/electrolyte. The optimized sample HGO1 has defect rich morphology, which is evident from the fact that crystal fringes were not observed. The defect rich carbon structure showed amorphous structure, without having defined crystal structure, as shown in Supplementary Fig. S2.

The crystal structure of the HGO synthesized via various etchants was analyzed using XRD. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), the characteristic peak of GO is observed around 10°, corresponding to the crystal plane (0 0 1) with a calculated lattice spacing of 8.3 Å. After etching, this peak is shifted to a higher angle due to partial reduction of GO during thermal treatment [25]. In addition, the intensity of this peak is related to the crystal size of GO. Compared with pristine rGO, the crystal size increases for HGO2 but decreases for HGO. These results are consistent with the SAED patterns, wherein HGO has slightly more defects. On the other hand, HGO2 has a lower content of amorphous domains because of the dissolution of amorphous carbon in the strong acid [25]. Amongst all samples, HGO1 has the lowest intensity in the GO characteristic peak, while its peak is centered around 25°, arising from the (0 0 2) graphitic plane of rGO. This shift indicates that a higher degree of reduction occurs in HGO1 during the hydrothermal treatment. Stronger etching and reducing nature of NH4OH leads to smaller crystallites with higher edge surfaces [26,27].

Structural defects of the rGO and HGO samples were compared using Raman spectra. Fig. 5 (b) shows two prominent peaks around 1350 cm−1 and 1604 cm−1 related to D and G bands, respectively. D band arises from the disordered carbons, accommodating maximum defects in their structure. While G band is associated with the ideally crystalline graphitic arrangement of carbon. The ID/IG value is tabulated in Fig. 5 (b), showing the highest defect content in HGO1. These defects originate from the surface functional groups decorated on the edges of the holes and GQDs [28]. The extremely small size of GQDs and holes in HGO1 (as confirmed by XRD) offer highly pendant surfaces to accommodate functional groups. Defect sites serve as active centers for catalytic reactions to improve catalytic activity. For the other HGO derivatives, the ID/IG values of HGO and HGO2 remain unchanged compared with rGO. It suggests that the oxidation through etchant and thermal reduction due to temperature is also occurring at the same time [28]. Therefore, the ID/IG value of HGO and HGO2 was almost similar to GO. It is interesting to note that, despite the thermal reduction, the defects of both HGO and HGO2 were retained because of hole formation despite the thermal reduction.

The chemical composition of the modified HGO was probed using XPS. Fig. 6 (a) shows three elemental peaks at 285, 400, and 533 eV related to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. Minor content of N1s was observed in all samples because of hydrazine monohydrate used to complete the reduction [29]. Similar elemental compositions of carbon and oxygen are detected in rGO, HGO, and HGO2. On the other hand, HGO1 shows a higher carbon ratio and nitrogen doping (5.5 %), as tabulated in Fig. 6 (a). HNO3 and NH4OH serve as nitrogen doping sources, whereas NH4OH has higher strength for both N-doping and reduction [29]. Therefore, higher content of carbon and nitrogen are detected in HGO1. Higher nitrogen content arises from the pendant surfaces with dangling bonds on the edges. Hence, nitrogen moieties are attached to these edges and enhance the functionality of the HGO1 and GQDs mixture.

At high resolution the carbon skeleton of the samples was consisting of C1s graphene, C-C, C-O, CO, and satellite (π-π * transitions of conjugated system), as shown in Fig. S3. Amongst all, the rGO showed the highest content of C-O (Fig. S3(a)), whereas the intensity of this bond was slightly decreased for HGO and HGO2 (Fig. S3(b, d)). This can be related to the thermal reduction of the respective samples during hydrothermal treatment at high temperatures. In contrast to all samples, highest C-C and C-H bond was observed in HGO1, proving that highest thermal reduction occurred in the presence of NH4OH.

Brunauer Emmett Teller (BET) analysis was used to measure the surface area of the composite electrode. The isotherm of nitrogen adsorption and desorption of freeze-dried HGO and GQDs mixture is shown in Fig. 6 (b). The surface areas of rGO, HGO, HGO1, and HGO2 are 7.61, 15.29, 1.71, and 37.46 m2/g, respectively. A similar trend in the intensity of the isotherm curves was observed. Only initial 0–0.3 pressure range was tested because of saturation, and only using this initial curve, BET surface area was predicted [30]. The higher surface area of HGO2 is related to its folded morphology, which inhibits the stacked structure. In contrast, HGO1 has stronger restacking due to stronger interactions of functional groups; thus, the surface area is lower [30]. The pores formed during etching cannot increase the surface area due to strong stacking of the nano-sheets during film formation.

To elucidate the reason of low surface area of these etched samples, we tested one sample after removing the GQDs. The HGO1 (without NGQDs) showed the surface area of 34.00 m2/g and non-linear adsorption desorption curve Fig. S4. Compared to composite structure of HGO1, with the surface area of 1.71 m2/g, a drastic increase in the surface area suggests that NGQDs serve as a binder between the etched sheets and block their pores, thus reducing the surface area.

The size and doping define the structure of GQDs and HGO and their optical properties. The optical properties of the composite are essential to estimate the suitability of composite structure for photocatalytic applications. The effect of etchant on the optical properties was evaluated by UV-Visible and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), the UV-Vis spectra have the first high energy peak in deep UV region (around 300 nm) with high energy. This energy absorption is related to the π-π * transition of electrons in conjugated carbon domains [31]. All rGO derivatives contain the carbon centers responsible for such excitations and this high energy peak is observed in all samples. In contrast, a low energy absorption spectrum (330 nm and higher) is associated with the n-π * transitions [32]. These excitations are produced from the doped impurities or dangling surface functional groups; therefore, higher absorption is observed for the composite structure with higher functionality (Fig. 7 (a)). Functional groups create intermediate energy levels between conduction and valance bands. These newly formed energy levels offer low energy sites for electron transitions, facilitating the electron transitions at low energy [33]. Amongst all etchants, NH4OH etched sample has higher absorption, which is directly related to its higher nitrogen doping and functionality. Excellent light absorbance is essential for photocatalytic applications [34,35]. The difference in optical absorption is also evident in GQDs and HGO colloidal solutions, as shown in the inset of Fig. 7 (a).

To confirm the formation of GQDs, the fluorescence was studied and shown in Fig. 7 (b). The PL signal shows two peaks associated with GQDs and HGO, centered at 490 nm and 580 nm, respectively. High PL intensity is related to the higher concentration of GQDs and high quantum yields [36]. The PL intensity of HGO1 is the highest for both emission peaks. In contrast, rGO and HGO2 only show a peak at 580 nm related to nano-sheets, whereas the greenish-blue emission signal of was not detected. The higher emission of GQDs formed in HGO1 is related to its higher nitrogen doping. Doped nitrogen increases the density of electrons near the Fermi level and promotes direct radiative recombination in the GQDs. High quantum yield is achieved by limiting the indirect recombination and restricting the pathways available for the recombination [37,38]. Single pathway for recombination results in longer dwell time and better photocatalytic activity [37]. The emission peaks associated with GQDs are broad due to their wide size distribution. In contrast, the surface functionality or surface states are responsible for the emission intensity. This difference in emission can also be observed in colloidal solutions of GQDs and HGO mix under UV light with a wavelength of 365 nm (inset of Fig. 7 (b)). In addition to the increase in the intensity of greenish-blue light emission signal, the PL intensity for the HGO1 at 580 nm was also increased. This was due to enhanced quantum confinement in the etched HGO1 structure, as crystal size is decreased after etching. Also, Foster resonance energy transfer between NGQDs and HGO1 also exists, which enhanced the quantum yield of the system.

Metal-oxides (ZnO, TiO2, etc.) are conventional photocatalysts functioning only under UV light. Broad light absorbance of carbon materials allows them to harvest the full solar spectrum for catalytic applications. However, the fast recombination of electron/hole pairs limits their catalytic applications. The charge separation of rGO can be tuned by controlling the size of the sheets [39]. The characteristics of semiconductors are confirmed in GQDs and HGO mixes for photocatalytic applications. Furthermore, suitable light absorbance and fluorescence characteristics are evident for photocatalytic applications [40]. Therefore, we tested the performance of the electrode for methylene blue (MB) degradation. The fabric sample of 10 cm2 was dipped in 10 mL of dye solution (10 mg/L). The saturated adsorption of the electrodes was calculated after immersion for 30 min in contaminated water. Afterward, UV light (360 nm, 4×12W, Phillips) and indoor room light (300–800 nm) were turned on. The dye concentration was then tested every 30 min for 240 min. In addition, the solution was kept under continuous stirring to ensure that photocatalysis was not diffusion-controlled.

The photocatalytic degradation results after adsorption are shown in Fig. 8. rGO showed an initial decrease in concentration due to adsorption. However, after 30 min, the dye concentration started to increase due to evaporation of water by photothermal effect of rGO. In contrast, the photocatalytic degradation was observed in all HGO samples, as GQDs serve as an active material. For instance, the HGO causes a 67 % decrease in the dye concentration, most likely due to photocatalysis by GQDs in HGO. Photothermal effect and charge mobility of HGO sheets may contribute minorly to dye degradation [41]. The HGO1 shows 95 % dye degradation characteristics, inferring that the GQDs formed in HGO1 has best photocatalysis. As expected, lowest photo-activity of 50 % was observed for the HGO2, due to least content of GQDs formed in its mix. It can be assumed that the role of adsorption is negligible, as HGO1 with lowest surface area (as measured by BET) showed best activity. The curves of dye degradation at 4 h time is given in Fig. 8 (b), showing a similar difference in concentration of dye concentration. The inset of Fig. 8 (b) shows the sample images after 4 h of photodegradation, showing similar dye degradation of the samples.

In addition to MB dye, the optimized sample HGO1 was also tested for methyl orange (MO) dye, showing 85 % of dye degradation in 3 h. This degradation can also be observed in the pictorial degradation images added in Fig. S5(a). The spectral changes at 1 and 2 h showed a skewness in the curve shape, as shown in Fig. S5. Significant degradation of MO dye confirms that the proposed electrode is suitable for the degradation of strong organic molecules.

Higher photocatalysis of GQDs from HGO1 is due to their nitrogen doping and suitable size. Both parameters strongly influence the photocatalysis of GQDs as they change surface reactivity and charge confinement. The most crucial factor for photocatalysis for N-doped GQDs is their high light absorption and low HOMO-LUMO gap [42]. Higher light absorption is responsible for excessive photoexcitation and more effective photocatalysis. In addition, the doped nitrogen serves as a n-type moiety, whereas oxygenated edges act as p-type domain. This p-n junction facilitates charge separation by accommodating electrons and holes in nitrogenated and oxygenated domains, respectively [43,44]. Fast charge recombination is suppressed in the junction structure, thus increasing the dwell time of excitons. Suppression of charge recombination allows the exciton to participate in the photodegradation for longer time [45]. Moreover, the nitrogen-doped structure has higher electron density because the lone pair of electrons from nitrogen is also involved in the conjugated system. Therefore, the non-radiative recombination paths are blocked, and only radiative recombination are promoted. Limited recombination paths extend the separation time of excitons, enhancing the photocatalytic activity [46]. Apart from N-doping and chemical composition, the size of GQDs is also critical in determining their photocatalysis. Smaller GQDs offer larger surface area and higher surface reactivity, leading to enhanced activity in HGO1-GQDs [39].

It is important to mention that the role of substrate is also important in the photocatalysis, wherein the rGO substrate provides high mobility of charge. HGO also serves as cocatalyst participating in the photocatalysis via photothermal effect. In addition, the carbon fabric substrate provides more pathways for charge flow, suppressing the charge recombination [13,47]. The porous structure of the fabric provides high roughness for light entrapment. In addition, larger interface is available for the interaction of pollutant dye and active electrode materials. Compared to the mobile phase, the photocatalytic efficiency of the active materials is drastically lower in passivated state [47]. However, careful selection of materials and electrode fabrication process can optimize the efficiency. The advantage of using active materials in passivated form is to reduce secondary pollution and enable recyclability.

For the bandgap and electronic structure analysis, we added the UPS data in manuscript as below as shown in Fig. 9. The optical properties and electronic structure of proposed carbon materials were examined using ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) measurements. The high binding energy near the valence band edge (Ecut off) derived from linear extrapolation to zero at the yield of secondary electrons and photoemission onset region (Eonset) relative to the Fermi level (EF) for all the samples are shown in Fig. 9. The work function of the samples was calculated using Eq. 1.
(1)
Work Function = h υ −
E
cutoff
where hυ = 21.22 eV is the incident photon energy and Eonset is the photoemission onset relative to the Fermi energy of Au at 0 eV. Ecut off for the rGO, HGO, HGO1, and HGO2 was around 21.13, 18.80, 18.50, 21.10 eV. By using these values, the calculated work function for the materials was approximated to be 0.09, 2.42, 2.72, and 0.12 eV, respectively. The characteristics of HGO2 and GO were similar, with respect to higher conductivity and zero bandgap. On the other hand, HGO and HGO1 showed semiconductor like nature, hence showing better photocatalytic activity. UPS data shows that the suitable charge separation in the semiconductor like nature of HGO1 provides longer dwell time of exciton to participate in photocatalytic reaction.

Dual nature of carbon materials for both photocatalysis and electrocatalysis is attractive for their diverse applications. Electrocatalysts are critical for emerging electrochemical applications, such as water splitting, supercapacitors, and battery devices. Electrocatalytic activity is assessed based on the cyclic voltammetry (CV) results. As shown in Fig. 10 (a), iodine based dummy electrolyte containing 10 mM LiClO4, 10 mM LiI, and 1 mM iodine in acetonitrile was used to evaluate the electrocatalytic activity with a scan rate of 10 mV/sec and a potential window from − 0.2–1 V.

Different electrodes showed two main peaks in the CV curves at positive and negative potentials related to oxidation and reduction reactions, respectively. The intensity of these peaks reflects the redox reaction capacity, whereas the voltage difference between the two peaks is peak to peak potential (EPP). A redox peak with high intensity suggests that a large number of active species are reduced/oxidized at a certain voltage. Whereas EPP is associated with the potential window required to activate the electrocatalyst. Ideally, an excellent electrocatalyst has a higher current density and a lower EPP.

Comparing the CV curves, rGO electrode show lowest activity with negligible reduction peak. In contrast, the current densities are significantly improved for all electrodes with HGO and GQDs mixtures. Comparatively, the difference in the EPP was insignificant. HGO1 shows better electrocatalysis because of its higher surface reactivity and conductivity. Higher conductivity leads to lower current losses in charge transport and better activity. Higher carbon content (as observed in XPS) is responsible for better conductivity of HGO1. In addition, despite the low surface area, the surface reactivity of HGO1 is significantly high, which is related to surface charge redistribution caused by nitrogen doping [48]. Due to the high electronegativity of nitrogen, negative charge is developed in nitrogenated domains. At the same time, the neighboring carbon is dominated by positive charge due to electron deficiency. Compared to neutral surface, the charged surface of nitrogen doped carbon has the tendency to attract and react with the electrolyte species [48,49]. Dangling bonds or surface defects in HGO1 (confirmed by Raman analysis) accelerate the reaction. Defect sites serve as active centers for the charge transfer and storage reactions. Specifically, the defects related to nitrogen are critical in boosting the electrocatalysis [50].

In addition, the electrocatalytic stability of the optimized electrode was tested at different scan rates, i.e. 2–20 mV/sec, as shown in Fig. S6(a). The electrode retained its peaks at high scan rates, confirming that suitable porosity and electrocatalytic activity is there. The intensity of peaks was increased, and peaks were shifted to higher potentials, which is commonly observed featured in carbon materials. In addition, the HGO1 electrode was subjected to continuous 50 cycles at 20 mV/sec, to confirm the cyclic stability (Fig. S69b)). Even after consecutive continuous cycles, there was not a significant loss in the catalytic activity, hence the stability of electrode can be confirmed. Such stability is important for longer lifetime of the electrochemical devices.

Thermal conductivity of the electrodes was tested to evaluate its suitability for heat dissipation applications. All electrodes were placed on a hot plate for 10 min and the image of the electrodes was taken using a thermal camera (Fig. 10 (b)). HGO and GQDs mixtures have larger contours than the rGO electrode. The brightest color contour in HGO1, reflecting its better heat absorption ability. This is also validated by placing the HGO1 electrode on a heated plate and recording its temperature after 15 min. Compared to bare rGO electrode (Fig. 10 (c)), the temperature of HGO1 electrode (Fig. 10 (d)) was 14 % higher. A significant increase in the temperature under the same condition is due to better thermal conducting network in the composite structure [51]. Larger scale networks are formed by the HGO and smaller GQDs further strengthens these connections as a filler. The compact structure of HGO (as observed from the BET analysis) can also prompt heat flow.

Herein, low boiling point etchants (H2O2, NH4OH, and HNO3) were used to etch rGO to obtain a mixture of HGO and GQDs for photo- and electro- active electrode fabrication. NH4OH-assisted etching provides better surface functionality and a defect-rich structure, especially the developed nitrogen-doped structure was responsible for better light absorbance, florescence, and associated photocatalysis. Better charge generation and channeling of composite structure resulted in complete dye degradation, compared to negligible degradation of pristine rGO electrode. In addition, charge transport and charge transfer characteristics were also improved when NH4OH was used as the etchant, resulting in 95 % dye degradation compared to negligible photocatalysis of rGO. The superior electrocatalytic activity of the electrode could be utilized for energy harvesting and storage applications.

Kyung Chul Sun: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Mumtaz Ali: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation. Iftikhar Ali Sahito: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Jung Woo Noh: Validation, Writing and Analysis assistance. Sung Hoon Jeong: Supervision, Project administration.
